refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation by the tele-operator.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability, and (2) ensuring latency is compatible with safety. The refined version separates the functional intent (enable teleoperation communication) from the performance constraint (latency compatible with safe operation). The phrase 'compatible with safe remote operation' remains in the FR because it defines the functional boundary—the system must deliver latency sufficient for the tele-operator to safely command the equipment—without prescribing the specific numerical threshold (150 ms p95), which belongs in acceptance criteria or design constraints, not the FR itself. The FR is now atomic: it states a single functional obligation in the problem domain.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (sensor input → tele-operator perception → command transmission → equipment actuation response) under agreed network conditions. Validate that measured latency permits the tele-operator to execute representative safety-critical tasks (e.g., obstacle avoidance, precision positioning) without loss of control or safety margin, as defined in the worksite safety plan.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit tele-operator commands to the remote equipment with measurable end-to-end latency.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall provide tele-operator feedback (sensor data, video, status) from the remote equipment with measurable end-to-end latency.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement a deterministic, low-latency communication pipeline with end-to-end latency budgeting across acquisition, encoding, transmission, decoding, and actuation stages, with active monitoring and adaptive quality-of-service controls to maintain p95 ≤ 150 ms under specified network conditions.

DP2: Establish a dual-channel architecture (primary high-fidelity + secondary low-latency fallback) with automatic switchover logic, enabling graceful degradation when primary path latency exceeds threshold, ensuring operator safety during transient network congestion.

DP3: Decompose teleoperation into independent functional streams (command path, feedback path, safety heartbeat) with separate latency budgets and prioritization rules, allowing each stream to be optimized and monitored independently without cross-coupling.

DP4: Employ edge-local processing (compute near acquisition and actuation points) to minimize round-trip latency by reducing payload size, eliminating unnecessary hops, and enabling local safety interlocks that do not depend on remote operator acknowledgment.

DP5: Implement a time-synchronized, predictive state model at both operator and remote site, reducing effective latency perception by allowing the operator to command based on predicted future state rather than delayed feedback, with continuous reconciliation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3 (Decompose teleoperation into independent functional streams with separate latency budgets and prioritization rules) combined with DP1 (deterministic low-latency pipeline with end-to-end budgeting and adaptive QoS) as the primary design approach, with DP4 (edge-local processing) as a supporting tactic.

potential_sources: DP3 + DP1 integration: Command path and feedback path must share underlying network infrastructure; risk of resource contention if not explicitly isolated (e.g., separate queues, priority tagging, or physical channels).
DP1 + DP4 coupling: Edge processing reduces payload but introduces local state management; must ensure state reconciliation logic does not create feedback loops that increase latency.
Safety heartbeat (DP3) vs. adaptive QoS (DP1): Heartbeat must never be subject to adaptive degradation; requires explicit priority guarantee and separate budget allocation.
Operator prediction model (DP5 alternative): If adopted, creates tight coupling between operator interface and remote state model; deferred to later design phase pending feasibility assessment.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 + DP1 combination is preferred because stream independence allows latency budgets to be allocated and monitored separately, reducing cross-domain coupling. DP4 (edge processing) is orthogonal and can be applied selectively to high-frequency feedback loops without affecting command path logic. Coupling risks are manageable through explicit interface contracts (e.g., priority levels, timeout semantics, state reconciliation protocols).

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: 'Agreed network conditions' are well-defined and stable (e.g., bandwidth, jitter, packet loss rate). RISK: If network conditions are variable or poorly characterized, latency budgeting will be unreliable. Mitigation: Require explicit network SLA or characterization study before detailed design.

ASSUMPTION: Operator cognitive load and reaction time are compatible with 150 ms p95 latency. RISK: If operator requires sub-100 ms latency for safe operation (e.g., high-speed manipulation), current FR may be insufficient. Mitigation: Validate acceptance criteria with human factors analysis and operational scenarios.

ASSUMPTION: Local safety interlocks (DP4) can be implemented without requiring remote operator approval for every action. RISK: If regulatory or operational policy mandates remote operator oversight for all safety-critical actions, edge processing may be constrained. Mitigation: Align DP4 with worksite safety plan and operational policies (noted in constraints).

ASSUMPTION: End-to-end latency can be measured and monitored continuously. RISK: If measurement infrastructure is absent or introduces overhead, adaptive QoS (DP1) cannot function effectively. Mitigation: Include telemetry and instrumentation as a supporting DP.

ASSUMPTION: Fallback to lower fidelity (DP2 alternative) is operationally acceptable. RISK: If mission-critical tasks require full fidelity, fallback may not be viable. Mitigation: Define fallback scenarios and operator training requirements.

ASSUMPTION: Network infrastructure supports prioritization (e.g., QoS tagging, separate channels). RISK: If network is best-effort only, DP3 stream isolation may not be enforceable. Mitigation: Verify network capabilities early; consider hybrid approaches (e.g., redundant paths, local buffering).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) the definition of 'safe remote operation' in terms of operator cognitive load, reaction time, and acceptable failure modes; (2) the scope of 'agreed network conditions' (e.g., bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, geographic distance); (3) whether local safety interlocks are permitted or all safety decisions must be operator-approved; (4) the role of fallback modes and operator training in meeting the latency requirement. Additionally, introduce a secondary FR addressing telemetry and monitoring: 'The NGHE solution shall provide continuous visibility into end-to-end latency and network health to enable operator decision-making and system diagnostics.' This supports DP1 (adaptive QoS) and reduces risk of latency violations going undetected.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates the decomposition of teleoperation into independent functional streams (command, feedback, safety monitoring) with separate latency budgets, prioritization rules, deterministic low-latency pipeline orchestration, end-to-end QoS adaptation, and coordination with edge-local processing for time-critical operations.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and high-level task directives; Teleoperation module decomposes these into prioritized functional streams with assigned latency budgets.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs vehicle state, sensor feedback, and latency/QoS metrics; supports adaptive feedback loop for operator awareness and DP1 QoS adjustment.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and alerts from Safety Services; Teleoperation enforces safety-aware prioritization rules and may escalate safety-critical events.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Manages deterministic low-latency transmission; Teleoperation coordinates stream scheduling, adaptive QoS negotiation, and edge offload decisions with network layer.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. DP3 (stream decomposition, prioritization, latency budgeting) and DP1 (deterministic pipeline, end-to-end QoS) are core teleoperation concerns. DP4 (edge-local processing) is a supporting tactic that Teleoperation orchestrates via Network Link and Vehicle interfaces. Minimal diffusion: safety rules are imported via SafetyAlert; network scheduling is coordinated but owned by Network Link; vehicle actuation is delegated to Vehicle module.

Poor fit. Vehicle owns actuation and local control loops, not teleoperation orchestration. DP3 (stream decomposition, prioritization) and DP1 (end-to-end latency budgeting) are teleoperation-level concerns that would diffuse across network and operator interface layers if placed here. DP4 (edge-local processing) is partially relevant but insufficient to justify containing the full DP.

Very poor fit. Safety Services owns constraint enforcement and alert generation, not teleoperation stream orchestration. DP3 and DP1 are orthogonal to safety service responsibilities. Placing them here would require Safety Services to own latency budgeting and QoS adaptation, creating severe coupling and diffusion across Teleoperation and Network Link.

Weak fit. NGHE Solution is a top-level system container. While it could logically contain Teleoperation as a subsystem, it is too broad to serve as the implementation boundary for DP3+DP1+DP4. Placing the DP here would obscure module ownership and diffuse responsibilities across multiple child modules (Teleoperation, Vehicle, Safety Services, Network Link). Better to use Teleoperation as the specific boundary.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: Teleoperation module (owned by teleoperation/control systems team or equivalent)
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP3 (stream decomposition, prioritization, latency budgeting) and DP1 (deterministic low-latency pipeline, end-to-end QoS adaptation) are inherently teleoperation-domain concerns. They define how operator commands and feedback are orchestrated across the system with time-critical guarantees. DP4 (edge-local processing) is a supporting tactic that Teleoperation coordinates via the Network Link interface and Vehicle module delegation. The Teleoperation module is the most specific boundary that contains the full DP without major diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: DP1 (end-to-end latency budgeting) requires tight coupling with Network Link and Vehicle modules. If network scheduling or vehicle actuation latencies are not observable/controllable by Teleoperation, the DP cannot be fully realized. Mitigation: Ensure Network Link and Vehicle expose latency metrics and accept budget constraints via well-defined interfaces.

Risk: DP3 (prioritization rules) may conflict with Safety Services constraints. If safety rules are not clearly communicated via SafetyAlert interface, Teleoperation may violate safety invariants. Mitigation: Establish formal contract between Teleoperation and Safety Services for constraint propagation.

Risk: DP4 (edge-local processing) assumes Vehicle module can execute offloaded computations. If Vehicle lacks sufficient compute or does not support the required algorithms, edge offload decisions made by Teleoperation will fail. Mitigation: Define edge compute capabilities and supported algorithms as part of Vehicle interface specification.

Risk: Adaptive QoS (DP1) requires real-time feedback on network and vehicle state. If Telemetry interface does not provide timely, accurate metrics, adaptation will be ineffective. Mitigation: Establish SLA for Telemetry latency and accuracy.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is recommended. DP3+DP1+DP4 is well-suited to the Teleoperation module boundary. However, to improve containment and reduce diffusion risk, recommend: (1) Explicitly define latency budget allocation and QoS negotiation protocols between Teleoperation and Network Link; (2) Formalize safety constraint propagation from Safety Services to Teleoperation via SafetyAlert; (3) Specify edge compute capabilities and algorithm support in Vehicle interface; (4) Establish Telemetry SLA for feedback loop closure in DP1 adaptive QoS.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The Teleoperation IM boundary is well-scoped and measurable against the acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms latency). The module's external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) provide clear observation points for end-to-end latency measurement and QoS feedback. Verification can be conducted via instrumented latency tracing across the pipeline and closed-loop adaptive QoS validation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR requirement maps unambiguously to the Teleoperation module as the primary owner of end-to-end teleoperation communication orchestration. Responsibility allocation is explicit: Teleoperation owns stream decomposition, latency budgeting, and QoS adaptation (DP3+DP1); Network Link owns transmission scheduling; Vehicle owns actuation and edge compute execution (DP4 support); Safety Services owns constraint enforcement. The SafetyAlert and Network Link interfaces establish clear responsibility boundaries.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 (independent functional streams with separate latency budgets and prioritization) combined with DP1 (deterministic low-latency pipeline with end-to-end budgeting and adaptive QoS) directly addresses the FR requirement for safe remote operation with controlled latency. DP4 (edge-local processing) is a supporting tactic that reduces latency for time-critical operations. The DP set is logically sound, non-contradictory, and sufficient to achieve the p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion when properly realized. No alternative DP set is recommended.

diffusion_risks: DP1 (end-to-end latency budgeting) diffusion risk: Teleoperation module depends on Network Link and Vehicle modules to expose latency metrics and accept budget constraints. If these modules do not provide timely, observable latency feedback or do not respect budget allocations, DP1 cannot be fully realized within Teleoperation. Mitigation: Establish formal SLA between Teleoperation and Network Link for latency observability and constraint acceptance; define Vehicle interface contract for actuation latency reporting.

DP3 (prioritization rules) diffusion risk: Safety-aware prioritization requires tight coupling with Safety Services. If SafetyAlert interface does not provide real-time, unambiguous safety constraints, Teleoperation may violate safety invariants or apply incorrect prioritization. Mitigation: Formalize safety constraint schema and propagation protocol in SafetyAlert interface specification; establish formal verification of constraint consistency.

DP4 (edge-local processing) diffusion risk: Teleoperation makes edge offload decisions but does not own edge compute execution. If Vehicle module lacks sufficient compute capacity, supported algorithms, or deterministic execution guarantees, offload decisions will fail or introduce unpredictable latency. Mitigation: Define edge compute capability matrix (CPU, memory, supported algorithms, execution latency bounds) as part of Vehicle interface; establish feedback loop for offload feasibility negotiation.

Adaptive QoS (DP1) feedback loop risk: DP1 requires real-time Telemetry feedback on network and vehicle state to drive QoS adaptation. If Telemetry interface latency exceeds the adaptation control loop period, feedback will be stale and adaptation ineffective. Mitigation: Establish Telemetry SLA with latency ≤ 50 ms (1/3 of p95 budget) and accuracy requirements for latency and QoS metrics.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (95% score). DP3, DP1, and DP4 are inherently teleoperation-domain concerns. The Teleoperation module boundary is the most specific and appropriate container for these DPs. Minimal diffusion (5%) is limited to: (1) safety constraint import via SafetyAlert (owned by Safety Services), (2) network scheduling coordination via Network Link interface (owned by Network Link), (3) edge compute execution via Vehicle interface (owned by Vehicle). These are appropriate delegations across well-defined interfaces, not diffusion. No alternative module boundary (Vehicle, Safety Services, NGHE Solution) would improve containment without introducing severe coupling and responsibility fragmentation.

recommended_revisions: Formalize latency budget allocation protocol: Define how Teleoperation decomposes the p95 ≤ 150 ms end-to-end budget across command path, feedback path, and safety monitoring path. Specify budget negotiation and reallocation rules with Network Link and Vehicle modules. Document in Teleoperation-Network Link and Teleoperation-Vehicle interface specifications.

Establish Network Link SLA: Define latency bounds, jitter limits, and QoS metric observability for the Network Link interface. Specify that Network Link must expose per-stream latency and packet loss metrics to Teleoperation within 50 ms for adaptive QoS feedback loop closure.

Formalize safety constraint propagation: Define SafetyAlert interface schema to include: (1) safety-critical stream identifiers, (2) hard latency bounds for safety-critical operations, (3) constraint priority levels, (4) constraint update frequency. Establish formal verification that Teleoperation's prioritization rules respect all safety constraints.

Specify edge compute capability matrix: Define Vehicle interface to expose: (1) available CPU/memory/storage, (2) supported algorithms and execution latency bounds, (3) deterministic execution guarantees, (4) offload feasibility negotiation protocol. Establish feedback loop for Teleoperation to query feasibility before making offload decisions.

Establish Telemetry SLA: Define latency (≤ 50 ms), accuracy (±5 ms for latency metrics), and update frequency (≥ 20 Hz) for Telemetry interface. Ensure Telemetry provides per-stream latency, QoS metrics, and network/vehicle state observations required for DP1 adaptive QoS.

Document DP4 edge offload decision logic: Specify the algorithm and heuristics Teleoperation uses to decide when to offload computation to Vehicle edge. Include fallback behavior if offload fails or latency exceeds budget. Ensure decision logic is deterministic and testable.

Add latency tracing and observability: Require instrumentation of the entire teleoperation pipeline (command ingestion, stream decomposition, prioritization, network transmission, vehicle execution, feedback aggregation) to enable end-to-end latency measurement and verification against p95 ≤ 150 ms criterion.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The Teleoperation module boundary is well-scoped, the DP set is logically sound and sufficient, and verification is feasible. However, the gate results expose five critical diffusion risks and six recommended revisions that are architectural in nature and require explicit sub-level decomposition to manage. Specifically: (1) DP1 (end-to-end latency budgeting) requires formalization of budget allocation and negotiation protocols across Network Link and Vehicle interfaces—this is a sub-FR concern; (2) DP3 (prioritization rules) requires formal safety constraint propagation and verification—a sub-FR concern; (3) DP4 (edge offload decisions) requires explicit decision logic and feasibility negotiation—a sub-FR concern; (4) Adaptive QoS feedback loop requires Telemetry SLA and observability instrumentation—sub-FR concerns. These are not minor implementation details but rather functional requirements at the next decomposition level that must be explicitly derived, verified, and allocated to sub-modules (e.g., LatencyBudgetManager, SafetyConstraintInterpreter, EdgeOffloadController, QoSAdaptationEngine, ObservabilityInstrumentation). Deriving these sub-FRs will: (a) make the diffusion risks explicit and manageable, (b) establish clear ownership and interface contracts, (c) enable verification of DP realization, (d) reduce architectural risk in a safety-critical system. The high safety criticality tailoring factor reinforces the need for explicit sub-level decomposition to ensure safety constraints are properly propagated and verified.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Allocate and manage end-to-end latency budget (p95 ≤ 150 ms) across command path, feedback path, and safety monitoring path, with dynamic reallocation based on network and vehicle state.

FR1.2: Interpret and enforce safety-critical stream constraints (hard latency bounds, priority levels, constraint updates) from Safety Services, ensuring Teleoperation prioritization rules respect all safety invariants.
FR1.3: Decompose teleoperation workload into independent functional streams (command, telemetry, safety alert) with separate latency budgets and QoS prioritization rules.

FR1.4: Decide when to offload computation to Vehicle edge compute (based on latency budget, edge capability, and feasibility) and execute offload with deterministic latency bounds and fallback behavior.

FR1.5: Adapt QoS parameters (stream prioritization, compression, sampling rates, transmission scheduling) in real-time based on Telemetry feedback (network latency, packet loss, vehicle state) to maintain p95 ≤ 150 ms.

FR1.6: Instrument and observe end-to-end teleoperation pipeline (command ingestion, stream decomposition, prioritization, network transmission, vehicle execution, feedback aggregation) to enable latency tracing and verification against p95 ≤ 150 ms criterion.

revise.why: The current FR, DP, and IM are sound and require no revision. The recommended revisions in the gate results are implementation-level details (SLA specifications, interface schemas, decision logic documentation) that will be addressed during sub-level decomposition and detailed design. No fundamental contradiction or insufficiency exists at the current level.

assumptions: Network Link module will expose per-stream latency and packet loss metrics to Teleoperation within 50 ms for adaptive QoS feedback loop closure.
Vehicle module will provide deterministic actuation latency bounds and edge compute capability matrix (CPU, memory, supported algorithms, execution latency bounds).
Safety Services will provide real-time, unambiguous safety constraints via SafetyAlert interface with formal schema and constraint priority levels.
Telemetry interface will provide latency ≤ 50 ms, accuracy ±5 ms for latency metrics, and update frequency ≥ 20 Hz.
Edge compute execution on Vehicle will support deterministic execution guarantees and feasibility negotiation for offload decisions.
Instrumentation and observability infrastructure will be available to enable end-to-end latency tracing and verification.
open_risks: DP1 diffusion risk: Teleoperation depends on Network Link and Vehicle to expose latency metrics and accept budget constraints. If these modules do not provide timely feedback or do not respect allocations, DP1 cannot be fully realized. Mitigation: Establish formal SLA between Teleoperation and Network Link for latency observability and constraint acceptance.

DP3 diffusion risk: Safety-aware prioritization requires tight coupling with Safety Services. If SafetyAlert interface does not provide real-time, unambiguous safety constraints, Teleoperation may violate safety invariants. Mitigation: Formalize safety constraint schema and propagation protocol in SafetyAlert interface specification.

DP4 diffusion risk: Teleoperation makes edge offload decisions but does not own edge compute execution. If Vehicle module lacks sufficient compute capacity or deterministic execution guarantees, offload decisions will fail. Mitigation: Define edge compute capability matrix and establish feedback loop for offload feasibility negotiation.

Adaptive QoS feedback loop risk: DP1 requires real-time Telemetry feedback. If Telemetry interface latency exceeds the adaptation control loop period, feedback will be stale and adaptation ineffective. Mitigation: Establish Telemetry SLA with latency ≤ 50 ms.

Safety constraint consistency risk: Multiple safety constraints from Safety Services may conflict or be inconsistent. Teleoperation prioritization rules may not be able to satisfy all constraints simultaneously. Mitigation: Establish formal verification of constraint consistency and define conflict resolution protocol.
evidence_needed: Formal latency budget allocation protocol and negotiation rules (to be derived in FR1.1 decomposition).
Network Link SLA specification with latency bounds, jitter limits, and QoS metric observability requirements.
SafetyAlert interface schema with safety-critical stream identifiers, hard latency bounds, constraint priority levels, and constraint update frequency.
Edge compute capability matrix specification (CPU, memory, supported algorithms, execution latency bounds, deterministic execution guarantees).
Telemetry SLA specification with latency (≤ 50 ms), accuracy (±5 ms), and update frequency (≥ 20 Hz) requirements.
DP4 edge offload decision logic specification (algorithm, heuristics, fallback behavior, determinism, testability).
Instrumentation and observability design for end-to-end latency tracing and verification.
Formal verification of Teleoperation prioritization rules against all safety constraints (to be derived in FR1.2 decomposition).
Closed-loop adaptive QoS validation test plan (to be derived in FR1.5 decomposition).
